title: Copper cleaning paste
subtitle: A scrubbing paste of bicarbonate of soda, table salt, and lemon juice for tarnished copper
excerpt: A scrubbing paste for cleaning tarnished copper pans, jugs, and ornaments. Made fresh per use from bicarbonate of soda, fine salt, and lemon juice. The mild acid lifts the oxide film; the salt is the gentle abrasive; the bicarbonate buffers the acid so it doesn't pit the metal. Polish with a dry cloth after.
sourceNotes: Lemon-and-salt copper cleaning is a traditional household technique. The mild citric acid attacks the copper oxide layer; the salt gives mechanical abrasion. Adding bicarbonate of soda buffers the acid mid-clean and prevents over-etching. Modern guides (e.g. Kew Gardens conservation team) describe this as the safe-for-display copper clean.

recipe.yieldDescription: Enough for one medium-sized copper pan or 3-4 small ornaments (mix fresh per use)
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix per use. The paste loses effectiveness within hours as the bicarbonate neutralises the lemon juice; the dry ingredients can be batched but the lemon juice goes in last.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Use the same day mixed. The reaction between the citric acid and the bicarbonate continues over hours and the paste loses cleaning power as it sits. Dry ingredients batched separately keep indefinitely.

glossaryTerms.definition: Copper oxidises in air to a thin dark layer of copper oxide and, with sulphur in urban air, copper sulphide. Mild acids (citric, acetic, tartaric) dissolve both. Mechanical scrubbing alone with a fine abrasive (salt, fine pumice) lifts the loosened oxide. The combination cleans faster than either alone.

Aggressive cleaners (concentrated acids, harsh abrasives) etch the surface. Repeatedly cleaning the same piece to mirror-shine strips microscopic copper each time and over decades visibly thins thin-walled antiques. Match cleaner aggressiveness to need: working pots need clean metal, decorative antiques benefit from leaving a slight patina.

content.text: Copper goes through three stages: bright pink from new or freshly cleaned, dull tan from weeks of air exposure, dark brown or green-tinged from months of neglect. All three respond to the same paste. The

is copper oxide and copper sulphide; the lemon's citric acid dissolves both, and the salt gives the mechanical lift. The bicarbonate buffers the acid mid-clean so it doesn't keep eating into the metal.

: working pots get a full clean before each big batch; decorative pieces benefit from a lighter pass that leaves a slight warm patina. Mirror-bright copper looks new for an hour and tarnishes faster the more polishes it's seen, because each polish removes a thin layer.

items.prepNote: About 1 tablespoon. Standard bicarbonate of soda.
About 1 tablespoon. Fine sea salt gives gentler abrasion than coarse rock salt; coarse can scratch soft copper.
Juice of one lemon. Strain out pips and pulp. Bottled lemon juice works but feels weaker.

content.text: Combine the bicarbonate of soda and salt in the mixing bowl. Squeeze in the lemon juice; the mix fizzes briefly as the citric acid meets the bicarbonate. Stir to a thick paste. If too dry, add a teaspoon of water; if too wet, a pinch more bicarbonate. Use within the hour.

Rinse the copper piece in warm water and shake off the excess. Scoop a teaspoon of paste onto a soft cotton cloth or a kitchen sponge. Rub in small circles across the surface; the cloth turns brown almost immediately as the tarnish lifts. Work over the whole piece, refreshing the paste as it loses colour. For deep tarnish, leave a smear of paste sitting for 2-3 minutes before rubbing.

Rinse the piece thoroughly in warm running water - the bicarbonate residue will dry to a white film if not rinsed off. Dry immediately with a soft cotton cloth, then buff with a second dry cloth to a low warm shine. Standing water on freshly cleaned copper will spot fast; dry every drop.

Do not use on lacquered copper - the lacquer prevents the acid from reaching the metal and the paste leaves a haze on the lacquer. Do not use on copper-plated items (zinc or steel under copper plating); aggressive cleaning lifts the plating. Skip on antique or fragile pieces of museum significance; conservation treatment is the right call there. Use household-safe gloves if your hands are sensitive to lemon juice; the citric acid stings on cuts.
